event: Early Archaic (Ortoiroid) peoples from South America become the first known human settlers on the island, leaving behind shell middens.
Saladoid-Barrancoid agricultural and ceramic-making Arawak peoples migrate from the Orinoco region and settle the island.
Arawak communities establish fishing and farming villages, cultivating cassava, cotton, and other crops across the island.
More warlike Kalinago (Carib) peoples arrive, displacing or absorbing the earlier Arawak inhabitants.
Christopher Columbus's arrival in the Caribbean ushers in the era of European exploration that will soon reach Barbados.
Portuguese explorer Pedro a Campos reportedly visits the island and names it Os Barbados ('the bearded ones'), likely after the bearded fig trees.
By the early 17th century the island is found largely uninhabited, its indigenous population having departed amid Spanish slave raids and disease.
An English ship captained by John Powell lands and claims the uninhabited island in the name of King James I.
The first English settlers, led by Henry Powell, arrive at Holetown (then Jamestown) to establish a permanent colony.
The House of Assembly is established, making it one of the oldest continuous parliamentary bodies in the Western Hemisphere.
Sugar cane cultivation is introduced from Dutch Brazil, transforming the island's economy toward large plantations.
The expanding sugar economy drives a rapid shift to enslaved African labor as the foundation of the plantation system.
Barbados submits to Parliamentary forces in the English Civil War, securing the Charter of Barbados guaranteeing self-government rights.
The Barbados Slave Code is enacted, legalizing brutal control over enslaved Africans and serving as a model for slave laws across the British colonies.
A major slave rebellion plot is uncovered and violently suppressed by colonial authorities.
George Washington visits Barbados, his only trip outside what would become the United States.
The Great Hurricane of 1780 devastates the island, killing thousands and destroying plantations and towns.
The British Slave Trade Act abolishes the transatlantic slave trade, ending the legal importation of enslaved Africans.
Bussa's Rebellion, the island's largest slave uprising, is brutally crushed but becomes a defining symbol of resistance.
Slavery is formally abolished in the British Empire, with formerly enslaved people entering a forced 'apprenticeship' period in Barbados.
Full emancipation takes effect as the apprenticeship system ends, freeing the remaining enslaved population.
A severe cholera epidemic sweeps the island, killing around 20,000 people.
The Confederation Riots erupt in opposition to a proposed federation with the Windward Islands.
Labour riots, sparked by economic hardship and the deportation of activist Clement Payne, lead to reforms and the rise of the trade union movement.
Grantley Adams founds the Barbados Labour Party, advancing the cause of workers and political reform.
Constitutional reforms expand the franchise and reduce property qualifications for voting.
Universal adult suffrage is granted, allowing all adults to vote regardless of property ownership.
Grantley Adams becomes the first Premier of Barbados under a new ministerial system of government.
Barbados joins the West Indies Federation, with Grantley Adams serving as its only Prime Minister.
Barbados attains full internal self-government, with Errol Barrow becoming Premier.
The West Indies Federation collapses, prompting Barbados to pursue independence on its own.
Barbados gains independence from the United Kingdom on 30 November, with Errol Barrow as its first Prime Minister.
Barbados is admitted to the United Nations and joins the Organization of American States as an independent state.
Barbados establishes diplomatic relations with Cuba and other nations, asserting an independent foreign policy.
Barbados becomes a founding member of the Caribbean Community (CARICOM).
Tom Adams leads the Barbados Labour Party to victory, becoming Prime Minister and ending the Democratic Labour Party's long rule.
Barbados supports and provides a staging base for the United States-led invasion of Grenada.
Errol Barrow, returned as Prime Minister, dies in office and is succeeded by Erskine Sandiford.
Owen Arthur becomes Prime Minister, beginning a long tenure marked by economic growth and tourism expansion.
A constitutional review commission recommends Barbados become a republic, signaling a long-term shift away from the monarchy.
The Caribbean Court of Justice is inaugurated with its seat in Bridgetown, replacing the British Privy Council for member states.
David Thompson leads the Democratic Labour Party to victory and becomes Prime Minister.
Prime Minister David Thompson dies in office and is succeeded by Freundel Stuart.
Historic Bridgetown and its Garrison are inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Mia Mottley becomes the first woman Prime Minister of Barbados after a landslide Barbados Labour Party victory.
Barbados becomes a parliamentary republic on 30 November, removing Queen Elizabeth II as head of state and installing Sandra Mason as its first President.
Mia Mottley wins re-election and gains international prominence for championing climate finance reform through the Bridgetown Initiative.
